Seven of Twelve

Music uses a grid of twelve notes, but rarely uses them all at once. The ring above rotates one common seven-note pattern. Tap for the drone and listen to the modes change.

Scroll down to read ↓

Part three ended with twelve equal notes per octave — a flat, frictionless grid where every key works identically. A cynic would predict that music, given a perfectly uniform set of notes, would use them uniformly. Instead, much Western tonal music picks seven of the twelve, builds from those, and treats the other five as outsiders — spices, accidents, visitors. Why subset at all? And why this strange, lopsided subset? 

the major scale, as semitone steps:   2 2 1 2 2 2 1   (W W H W W W H)

seven steps, summing to 12 — five whole steps, two halves,
and the halves are NOT evenly placed

The uneven spacing gives the scale structure. A melody is a path through a set of notes, and a path needs landmarks. If the notes are evenly spaced — play the whole-tone scale, six equal steps of 2 — every position sounds like every other; the ear loses its place; the music goes weightless (Debussy used exactly this for dream sequences). The two half steps in the major scale break the symmetry. They sit in unique positions, so every note in the scale has a different pattern of neighbours — every note is addressable. The ear always knows where it is, and above all it knows where home is.

Step 1 · The ringScales are step patterns

Stop thinking of a scale as a list of note names — think of it as a shape: a necklace of twelve positions with seven beads. The demo below draws C at the top and lights the major-scale pattern. The arcs between lit beads are the steps: cool blue-green for whole steps, hot orange for the two half steps. Play the scale, then play the riff, and watch the comet pass the orange arcs — those are the moments the melody feels like it's leaning somewhere.

Now press rotate the pattern. The beads jump — same necklace, same seven-bead shape, rotated one scale-degree around a root that refuses to move. With the drone holding C, each rotation puts the half steps in new places relative to home, and the mode changes. These rotations are the modes, and musicians sort them by brightness — by how high the pattern stacks its notes: 

Lydian      brightest   (the raised 4th — film-score wonder)
Ionian      the major scale — bright, settled, default
Mixolydian  major with a slouch — rock, folk, bagpipes
Dorian      minor but hopeful — "Scarborough Fair", So What
Aeolian     natural minor — melancholy proper
Phrygian    dark, Spanish, urgent — that ♭2 right above home
Locrian     so unstable it can't even hold its home note

Try the pattern button, too: pentatonic (five notes, no half steps at all — which is why you can improvise on the black keys with zero training and zero wrong notes), harmonic minor (with its exotic three-semitone leap), and the placeless whole-tone scale.

Aside · 40,000 years of pentatonic

The oldest playable instruments we've found — bone flutes from Geissenklösterle and Divje Babe, tens of thousands of years old — appear to sound pentatonic-ish sets, and the five-note scale turns up essentially everywhere humans made music: Chinese guqin repertoire, West African kora, Celtic ballads, Appalachian banjo, the blues. Bobby McFerrin's famous audience demo (look it up — "World Science Festival, pentatonic") shows a crowd of strangers singing the pattern perfectly, unprompted, jumping to notes he never taught. Whether that universality is hardwired or just the easiest gradient to roll down, no one fully knows — but no-half-steps means nothing clashes, and that travels.

Deep dive: why the diatonic shape, of all shapes?

Of the 66 ways to pick seven notes from twelve (up to rotation), why did this one conquer? It is bizarrely over-engineered. It is the unique 7-of-12 set that is maximally even — the beads spread as uniformly as possible without being uniform. It contains every interval type a different number of times (deep scale property), which is why each mode sounds distinct. Each scale member is reachable from the last by a fifth — the chain F→C→G→D→A→E→B is six fifths, so the scale is a connected segment of part three's spiral. And it's a useful compromise: enough asymmetry to give every note an address, enough evenness that melodies move in similar-sized steps. Pick almost many useful scale properties, and the diatonic set is at or near the optimum.

Step 2 · TranspositionKeys, and the map of them

Rotating the pattern against a fixed root gave modes. The other move: keep the pattern's orientation and slide the whole necklace to start somewhere else. Same shape, new home note — that's a key. D major is the major-scale pattern with its "C" relabeled "D". Thanks to part three's equal temperament, it sounds identical, just higher — twelve interchangeable homes, which raises a practical question: if you're in one key, how far away is another?

The circle of fifths provides a useful answer, and part three already drew it for us. Arrange the twelve keys by fifths — each key one fifth up from the last — and neighbours on the circle share six of their seven notes. Click around the map below: the rim glows by overlap with your chosen key. One step away (C → G): change one note. Across the circle (C → F♯): almost total strangers, two notes in common.

This is why the circle of fifths is on the wall of every practice room: it's not a list, it's a distance metric. Songs modulate to neighbouring keys because the listener barely has to move — six sevenths of their mental furniture stays put. A jump across the circle is a scene change, and composers spend it like the expensive effect it is. The key signatures fall out for free: each clockwise step adds one sharp (F C G D A E B, the same chain of fifths), each counter-clockwise step one flat.

Aside · The map predates the theory

The earliest known circle-of-fifths diagram is from Nikolai Diletsky's Grammatika (1670s), a composition manual for Muscovite church musicians, a generation before Johann David Heinichen printed the version Western theory descends from. Both were practical documents — "which keys can my choir reach without falling apart" — long before anyone proved theorems about maximally even sets. Musicians, as usual, shipped first and theorized later.

EpilogueA tonal landscape

Put the series so far in one stack: physics gave us intervals (part two), arithmetic forced the twelve-note grid (part three), and now a lopsided seven-note pattern has turned the flat grid into a landscape — a place with landmarks, neighbourhoods, and above all a home that every melody can measure its distance from. Play the riff in the ring demo one more time and end it on the second note instead of the first: feel the itch? That's the landscape exerting force.

So far, that pull only acts on one note at a time. The next post stacks notes three-deep — chords — and examines why major and minor differ, why one particular chord (the dominant) strongly implies a return home, and how common progressions use that effect.

Continued in Part five: Why Minor Sounds Sad — triads, tension, and the pull of the dominant: harmony as motion.

Sound by Tone.js. Standing on the shoulders of: Clough & Douthett (maximal evenness, 1991) · Diletsky (1670s) · every practice-room wall
